answer:
**The Wife's Lament** (ch. 3 of *Old English Elegies*, anonymous, Old English): The speaker, a woman exiled and confined to a cave under an oak tree after her husband's kin plotted to separate them, wants reunion with her husband, to whom she swore eternal vows.[^1] The text presents this as unattainable: she dwells joylessly alone, weeping endlessly through "paths of exile," while he suffers in a "dreary hall"; it ends with generalized woe for "the one who must in longing wait for the beloved," implying perpetual sorrow without resolution.[^2]

**Maa ka Khwab** ("Mother's Dream," ch. 29 of *Bang-E-Dara Part 1* by Allama Iqbal): In her dream, the mother—tormented by her son's absence—wants him to return home and care for her, as she strings "each day a garland of [her] tears" over his departure.[^3] The text reveals through the son's rebuke that she hinders his own path (he trails a procession of lamp-bearing boys because "your tears... have extinguished it"), suggesting her desire traps him; her possessiveness prevents both reunion and his success, with no fulfillment indicated.[^4]

[^1]: *Old English Elegies*, ch. 3 (*The Wife's Lament*), [p-66ceaf](/works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e/03-ik-this-giedd-wreche-bi-me-ful-geomorre/translation/#p-66ceaf).
[^2]: *Old English Elegies*, ch. 3 (*The Wife's Lament*), [p-956fe7](/works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e/03-ik-this-giedd-wreche-bi-me-ful-geomorre/translation/#p-956fe7).
[^3]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 1*, ch. 29 (*Maa ka Khwab*), [p-c4bed6](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c/29-maa-ka-khwab/translation/#p-c4bed6).
[^4]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 1*, ch. 29 (*Maa ka Khwab*), [p-4feffc](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c/29-maa-ka-khwab/translation/#p-4feffc).
